volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Persian (Section 4.3.516b - Section 4.4.581)

sections.label: Section 4.3.516b
sections.original: Istas , quae norunt , roga .
sections.translation: Ask that which knows : I know about as much as yourself , except that I was the first to read it through . But as you ’ ve begun , learn the matter from the letter .

sections.label: Section 4.3.518b
sections.original: Bene me mones .
sections.translation: You counsel me aright . Keep silence .

sections.label: Section 4.3.519b
sections.original: Nunc ad illud venies quod refert tua .
sections.translation: Now you ’ ll come to that which does relate to your interest .

sections.label: Section 4.3.520
sections.original: Iste quí tabellas adfert adduxit simul
sections.translation: The person that brings this letter , has taken with him a well-bred female of engaging charms , who has been stolen , and brought from the inmost parts of Arabia ; I wish you to take charge of her that she may be sold there ; but he who makes purchase of her , must buy her at his own risk ;

sections.label: Section 4.3.525
sections.original: mancipio neque promittet neque quisquam dabit .
sections.translation: nobody will promise or give a warranty . Take you care that he receives money full weight and counted . Pay attention to this , and give attention that the stranger is attended to . Farewell .

sections.label: Section 4.3.528
sections.original: Quíd igitur ? postquam recitasti quod erat cerae creditum ,
sections.translation: What then ? After you have read over what has been committed to the wax , do you believe me now ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.529b
sections.original: Vbi nunc illest hospes , qui hasce huc attulit ?
sections.translation: Where now is this stranger that brought this letter ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.530
sections.original: Iam hic credo aderit : arcessivit illam a navi .
sections.translation: He ’ ll be here just now , I believe ; he has sent for her from the ship .

sections.label: Section 4.3.530b
sections.original: Nil mi opust
sections.translation: I don ’ t want any lawsuits or quirks at all . Why should I be laying out so much money at such a distance ? Unless I get her on warranty , what need have I of this purchase ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.533
sections.original: Tacen an non taces ? numquam ego te tam esse matulam credidi .
sections.translation: Will you , or will you not , hold your tongue ? I never did believe you to be such a blockhead . What are you afraid of ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.534b
sections.original: Metuo hercle vero . sensi ego iam compluriens ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I really am afraid ; I ’ ve experienced it now so many times ,

sections.label: Section 4.3.535
sections.original: neque mi haud imperito eveniet , tali ut in luto haeream .
sections.translation: and it will not befall me without having already experienced it , to be getting stuck in such a quagmire .

sections.label: Section 4.3.536
sections.original: Nil pericli mihi videtur .
sections.translation: There seems to be no risk .

sections.label: Section 4.3.536b
sections.original: Scio istuc , sed metuo mihi .
sections.translation: I know that ; but I ’ m afraid about myself .

sections.label: Section 4.3.537
sections.original: Mea quidem istuc nil refert : tua ego hoc facio gratia ,
sections.translation: It matters nothing whatever to me , so far as I ’ m concerned ; it ’ s for your sake I mentioned it , that I might at the earliest moment give you an opportunity of advantageously purchasing her .

sections.label: Section 4.3.539_540
sections.original: Gratiam habeo . sed te de aliis , quam alios de te suaviust
sections.translation: I return you thanks ; but it ’ s a nicer thing for you to become wise through others , than for others through yourself .

sections.label: Section 4.3.541b
sections.original: Ne quis vero ex Arabia penitissuma
sections.translation: Surely no person can follow after her from the inmost parts of Arabia . Will you make purchase of her , then ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.542b
sections.original: Videam modo
sections.translation: Only let me see the commodity .

sections.label: Section 4.3.543b
sections.original: Aequa dicis . séd optume eccum ipse advenit
sections.translation: You say what ’ s fair . But look , most à propos , the stranger is coming himself , who brought this letter hither .

sections.label: Section 4.3.544b
sections.original: Hicinest ?
sections.translation: Is that he ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.544c
sections.original: Hic est .
sections.translation: That ’ s he .

sections.label: Section 4.3.545
sections.original: Haecine illást furtiva virgo ?
sections.translation: And is that the girl that was stolen ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.545b
sections.original: Iuxta tecum aeque scio ,
sections.translation: I know just about as well as yourself , except that I have seen her . Upon my faith , she certainly is genteel looking , whoever she is .

sections.label: Section 4.3.547
sections.original: Sat edepol concinnast facie .
sections.translation: Faith , she has pretty regular features .

sections.label: Section 4.3.547b
sections.original: Vt contemptim carnufex .
sections.translation: With what contempt the hang-dog does speak of her . ( To DORDALUS .) Let ’ s examine her beauty in silence .

sections.label: Section 4.3.548b
sections.original: Laudo consilium tuom .
sections.translation: I approve of your advice .

sections.label: Section 4.4.549
sections.original: Satin Athenae tibi sunt visae fortunatae atque opiparae ?
sections.translation: Doesn ’ t Athens seem to you a rich and opulent place ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.550
sections.original: Vrbis speciem vidi , hominum móres perspexi parum .
sections.translation: I ’ ve seen the appearance of the city ; the customs of the people I ’ ve observed but little of .

sections.label: Section 4.4.551
sections.original: Numquid in principió cessavit verbum docte dicere ?
sections.translation: At the very outset has she forborne to make a wise remark .

sections.label: Section 4.4.552
sections.original: Hau potui etiam in primo verbo perspiceré sapientiam .
sections.translation: I cannot by her very first words form an estimate of her wisdom .

sections.label: Section 4.4.553
sections.original: Quid id quod vidisti ? ut munitum muro tibi visum oppidumst ?
sections.translation: What as to that which you have seen ? How does the city seem fortified to you , with its wall ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.554
sections.original: Si incolae bene sunt morati , pulchre munitum arbitror .
sections.translation: If the inhabitants have good morals , I think it ’ s properly fortified .

sections.label: Section 4.4.555
sections.original: perfidia et pecúlatus ex urbe et avaritia si exulant ,
sections.translation: If Perfidiousness , and Peculation , and Avarice are exiled from the city , Envy in the fourth place , Ambition in the fifth , Scandal in the sixth , Perjury in the seventh .

sections.label: Section 4.4.557b
sections.original: Euge .
sections.translation: Bravo !

sections.label: Section 4.4.557c
sections.original: Octava indiligentia ,
sections.translation: Idleness in the eighth , Injustice in the ninth , Immorality , which is the very worst in its attack , in the tenth .

sections.label: Section 4.4.560
sections.original: ubi ea aderúnt , centumplex murus rebus servandis parumst .
sections.translation: If these things shall not be away from it , a wall a hundred-fold were too little for preserving its interests .

sections.label: Section 4.4.561
sections.original: Quid ais tu ?
sections.translation: What say you ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.561b
sections.original: Quid vis ?
sections.translation: What do you mean ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.561c
sections.original: Tu in illis es decem sodalibus :
sections.translation: You are among those ten companions ; you must depart in banishment from here .

sections.label: Section 4.4.562b
sections.original: Quid iam ?
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.562c
sections.original: Quia periurus es .
sections.translation: Because you are perjured .

sections.label: Section 4.4.563
sections.original: Verba quidem haud indocte fecit .
sections.translation: Really she has spoken not without some cleverness .

sections.label: Section 4.4.563b
sections.original: Ex tuo , inquam , usust : eme hanc .
sections.translation: That ’ s to your advantage , I say ; you buy her .

sections.label: Section 4.4.564
sections.original: Edepol qui cum hanc magis contemplo , magis placet .
sections.translation: Upon my faith , the more I look at her , the more she pleases me .

sections.label: Section 4.4.564b
sections.original: Si hanc emeris ,
sections.translation: If you do buy her ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.565
sections.original: di immortales , nullus leno te alter érit opulentior .
sections.translation: immortal Gods , no other Procurer will be more wealthy than yourself ; at your will you ’ ll be turning people out of their estates and households ; you ’ ll be transacting business with men of the highest rank ; they ’ ll be longing for your favour ; they ’ ll be coming to make merry at your house .

sections.label: Section 4.4.568b
sections.original: At ego intro mitti votuero .
sections.translation: But I shan ’ t allow them to be admitted .

sections.label: Section 4.4.569
sections.original: At enim illi noctu occentabunt ostium , exurent fores :
sections.translation: But then at night they ’ ll be singing [*] before your threshold , and be burning down your door ;

sections.label: Section 4.4.570
sections.original: proin tu tibi iubeás concludi aédis foribus ferreis ,
sections.translation: do you at once order your house to be fastened with a door of iron [*] , change for a house of iron , fix in thresholds of iron , a bar of iron and a ring ; if you don ’ t prove sparing of the iron , do you order thick fetters of iron to be rivetted upon yourself .

sections.label: Section 4.4.574
sections.original: I sis in malum cruciatum .
sections.translation: Away to utter perdition !

sections.label: Section 4.4.574b
sections.original: I sane tu , hanc eme atque ausculta mihi .
sections.translation: Go then , make purchase of her , and follow my advice .

sections.label: Section 4.4.575
sections.original: Modo uti sciam , quanti indicet .
sections.translation: Only let me know how much he asks for her .

sections.label: Section 4.4.575b
sections.original: Vin huc vocem ?
sections.translation: Should you like me to call him here ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.575c
sections.original: Ego illo accessero .
sections.translation: I ’ ll go to him .

sections.label: Section 4.4.576
sections.original: Quid agis , hospes ?
sections.translation: How fare you , guest ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.576b
sections.original: Venio , adduco hanc ad te , ut dudum dixeram .
sections.translation: I ’ m come ; I ’ ve brought her ( pointing to the DAMSEL .) as I just now said I would . For yesterday at night the ship arrived in harbour : I want her to be sold , if she can ; if she cannot , I intend to go away from here as soon as I can .

sections.label: Section 4.4.579
sections.original: Salvos sis , adulescens .
sections.translation: Greetings to you , young man .

sections.label: Section 4.4.579b
sections.original: Siquidem hanc vendidero pretio suo .
sections.translation: If indeed I shall dispose of her at her own price —

sections.label: Section 4.4.580
sections.original: At qui aut hoc emptore vendes pulchre , aut alio non potis .
sections.translation: Why , you ’ ll either sell her handsomely with him for your purchaser , or you can to no one .

sections.label: Section 4.4.581
sections.original: Esne tu huic amicus ?
sections.translation: Are you a friend of his ?
